metadata.description: Essential Chinese vocabulary for family relationships, describing people, and personal characteristics

content.name: that
content.side_g: 那 nà is the distal demonstrative ('that'). Before a measure word it's often pronounced nèi in colloquial Beijing speech, e.g. 那个 nèige. Pairs with 这 zhè ('this') as a core demonstrative contrast.

content.name: possessive_particle
content.side_g: 的 de marks possession and modification: [possessor]+的+[noun]. Usually dropped with close family/pronouns (我爸爸 'my dad'); keeping it (我的爸爸) adds emphasis or contrast. Also links adjectives to nouns: 大的照片.

content.name: photo
content.side_g: 照片 is a disyllabic noun; its measure word is 张 zhāng (used for flat things), not 个. Colloquially you may also hear just 照 in compounds like 照相 ('take a photo').

content.name: this
content.side_g: 这 zhè is the proximal demonstrative. Before a measure word it's often pronounced zhèi colloquially (这个 zhèige). Standard 'This is X' introductions use 这是… rather than 它是… (它 is rarely used for people).

content.name: father
content.side_g: 爸爸 is informal/affectionate, the default spoken word for 'dad.' The formal/written equivalent is 父亲 fùqīn. Note the neutral tone on the second 爸.

content.name: mother
content.side_g: 妈妈 is informal; the formal form is 母亲 mǔqīn. Second syllable is neutral tone. 爸爸/妈妈 pattern the same way as most family reduplications (哥哥, 姐姐, 弟弟).

content.name: measure_word
content.side_a: (measure word for many common everyday objects)
content.side_g: 个 is the default, most general measure word — use it when unsure. Originally 4th tone (gè) but in [Number+个+Noun] it regularly reduces to neutral tone: yí ge, liǎng ge. Triggers tone sandhi on 一 (yī→yí before 4th tone).

content.name: female
content.side_g: 女 is rarely used alone as a predicate; it attaches to nouns to form compounds: 女儿 'daughter,' 女孩子 'girl,' 女人 'woman.' Contrast with 男 nán.

content.name: child
content.side_g: 孩子 covers both 'child' (young person) and 'one's own offspring' regardless of age. Gender-specified forms: 男孩子 (boy), 女孩子 (girl). Second syllable is neutral tone.

content.name: who
content.side_g: Also pronounced shuí in formal/literary contexts; shéi dominates in speech. Chinese question words stay in situ — no inversion: 他是谁? literally 'He is who?' Doesn't take 吗 (question words and 吗 are mutually exclusive).

content.name: she
content.side_g: 她 (female 'she') and 他 (male 'he') are homophones — both tā — distinguished only in writing. The 女 radical on 她 marks feminine reference; a neologism from the early 20th century modeled on English.

content.name: older_sister
content.side_f: My older sister has a daughter.
content.side_g: 姐姐 = older sister only; younger sister is 妹妹 mèimei. Chinese obligatorily marks birth order in sibling terms. Can also be used as a polite address for a slightly older unrelated young woman.

content.name: male
content.side_g: 男 pairs with 女 and, like 女, doesn't stand alone as a predicate. Forms compounds: 男人 'man,' 男孩子 'boy,' 男朋友 'boyfriend.' For 'my son is a boy' you'd say 我儿子是男孩子, not *我儿子是男.

content.name: younger_brother
content.side_g: 弟弟 = younger brother only; older brother is 哥哥 gēge. Second syllable neutral-toned. Like 姐姐, age-relative sibling terms are grammatically required — there's no generic 'brother.'

content.name: he
content.side_g: 他 (male 'he') is homophonous with 她 (female 'she') — tā. 他 is also the default/generic pronoun when gender is unknown or mixed. Spoken Chinese makes no audible he/she distinction.

content.name: eldest_brother
content.side_f: My eldest brother has a son.
content.side_g: 大哥 literally 'big brother' = eldest brother specifically (vs. 二哥 second-oldest, etc.). Also used as a respectful address to an older man, or in triad/jianghu contexts for a 'boss.'

content.name: son
content.side_g: 儿 carries the core meaning 'son' but also functions as a diminutive suffix (花儿 huār). 儿子 refers to any son regardless of age. Parallel to 女儿 'daughter.'

content.name: have
content.side_g: 有 = 'to have' AND 'there is/are' (existential). Its negation is irregular: ONLY 没有, never *不有. 没 alone can also negate 有: 我没有 = 我没. Used for possession, existence, and (later) past aspect.

content.name: daughter
content.side_g: Written with an apostrophe in pinyin (nǚ'ér) to mark the syllable boundary before the vowel — without it, 'nüer' would be ambiguous. Parallels 儿子; the 女 prefix feminizes the base term 儿.

content.name: not
content.side_f: I don't have a younger brother.
content.side_g: 没 is the obligatory negator for 有: 没有, never 不有. More broadly 没(有) negates possession, existence, and completed actions; 不 negates everything else (general/habitual/future). 没 itself undergoes no tone sandhi.

content.name: gao_wenzhong
content.side_f: Gao Wenzhong has an older sister.
content.side_g: 高 is the surname (placed first, as in all Chinese names); 文中 is the two-character given name. A recurring textbook character in Integrated Chinese. When addressing, full name or 高先生 is polite; 文中 alone is intimate.
